description: This bar graph quantifies the individual contributions of five distinct strengthening mechanisms to the yield strength (YS) of Fe-6.8Al ferritic low-density steel under three processing conditions: 98% cold rolling (CR), and isothermal annealing at 750°C for 5 min and 60 min. The three stacked bars represent total yield strengths of 1800 MPa, 700 MPa, and 265 MPa, respectively. The legend identifies the mechanisms as dislocations (magenta), grain boundary (blue), ordering (green), solid solution (black), and lattice frictional stress (red). In the 98% CR state, dislocation strengthening and grain boundary strengthening provide the dominant contributions, consistent with the high dislocation density (~10^15 m^-2) and fine crystallite size (~102 nm) reported in the text. Upon annealing, the contributions from dislocations and grain boundaries decrease significantly due to recovery/recrystallization and grain growth (to 25.2 µm at 5 min and 48.3 µm at 60 min), while solid solution and ordering contributions remain relatively stable. This figure mechanistically explains the dramatic strength reduction from severe work hardening to softened recrystallized states.
